questions.question:
For the composition table of a cyclic group shown below:

  * | a  b  c  d
  --+------------
  a | a  b  c  d
  b | b  a  d  c
  c | c  d  b  a
  d | d  c  a  b

Which one of the following choices is correct?
A. a, b are generators
B. b, c are generators
C. c, d are generators
D. d, a are generators
questions.to_find: Which two elements generate the whole cyclic group of order 4

understand.plain: A generator of a cyclic group of order n is an element whose powers run through all n elements before returning to the identity (i.e. its order equals n). First spot the identity from the table, then take repeated products of each element and see which ones take 4 steps to come back to the identity. Those are the generators.

keywords.explain: The element e for which e*x = x*e = x for every x; its row in the table just copies the column headers.
keywords.example: Row a reads a b c d, identical to the header, so a is the identity.

keywords.explain: The smallest positive integer k with x^k = e. In a cyclic group of order n, a generator is exactly an element of order n.
keywords.example: c*c = b, c^3 = d, c^4 = a (= e), so order(c) = 4.

keywords.explain: An element whose successive powers produce every element of the group, so it 'generates' the whole group.
keywords.example: Since order(c) = order(d) = 4 = |G|, both c and d are generators.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='16' y='28'>Powers of c cycle through all 4 elements:</text><circle cx='70' cy='90' r='26' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='62' y='96'>c</text><circle cx='190' cy='90' r='26' class='bg'/><text class='ink lbl' x='182' y='96'>b</text><circle cx='310' cy='90' r='26' class='bg'/><text class='ink lbl' x='302' y='96'>d</text><circle cx='430' cy='90' r='26' class='ok'/><text class='ink lbl' x='418' y='96'>a=e</text><line x1='96' y1='90' x2='164' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='216' y1='90' x2='284' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='336' y1='90' x2='404' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text class='mut sub' x='112' y='80'>*c</text><text class='mut sub' x='232' y='80'>*c</text><text class='mut sub' x='352' y='80'>*c</text><text class='ink sub' x='16' y='160'>c^1=c, c^2=b, c^3=d, c^4=a  => order 4 => generator</text><text class='ink sub' x='16' y='185'>d behaves the same way; b^2=a so order(b)=2 (not a generator).</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A chain showing c, c^2=b, c^3=d, c^4=a=identity, illustrating that c (and likewise d) has order 4 and so generates the group.

given.aim: Identify the elements of the order-4 cyclic group whose order equals 4, since those are precisely the generators.

terms.meaning: A cyclic group of order 4 defined by the given Cayley (composition) table.
terms.example: Its operation * is read off the table, e.g. c*d = a.
terms.connects: Being cyclic of order 4 means at least one element has order 4.

terms.meaning: The neutral element whose row reproduces the header a b c d.
terms.connects: Reaching a via repeated multiplication signals a full cycle back to the start.

terms.meaning: Smallest k with x^k = a; a generator needs order(x) = 4.
terms.connects: Only elements of order 4 generate the whole group.

given.plan: Find the identity, compute the order of each element by repeated self-multiplication, and pick the elements whose order is 4.

steps.title: Find the identity
steps.apply: Row a is a b c d, identical to the column headers, so a is the identity element e.
steps.note: A full cycle ends back at a.

steps.title: Order of b
steps.apply: b*b = a = e, so order(b) = 2. An order-2 element cannot generate a 4-element group.

steps.title: Order of c and d
steps.apply: c^2 = c*c = b, c^3 = c*b = d, c^4 = c*d = a = e, so order(c) = 4 = |G|. Similarly d*d = b, d^3 = d*b = c, d^4 = a, so order(d) = 4.
steps.note: Both c and d run through all four elements.

solution.result: c and d are the generators, so option C is correct.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that in a cyclic group of order n the generators are exactly the elements of order n; here c and d have order 4 while b has order 2.

questions.question:
Consider the set S = {1, omega, omega^2}, where omega and omega^2 are cube roots of unity. If * denotes the multiplication operation, the structure (S, *) forms
A. A Group
B. A Ring
C. An integral domain
D. A field
questions.to_find: What algebraic structure (S, *) is, with S the cube roots of unity under multiplication

understand.plain: We have only one operation here, multiplication, acting on the three cube roots of unity {1, omega, omega^2}. A ring, integral domain, and field all need TWO operations (addition and multiplication), so they are ruled out immediately. With a single operation that is closed, associative, has identity 1, and gives every element an inverse, the structure is a group.

keywords.explain: The three solutions of x^3 = 1, namely 1, omega, omega^2, where omega = e^(2*pi*i/3) and omega^3 = 1.
keywords.example: omega * omega^2 = omega^3 = 1, so omega and omega^2 are inverses of each other.

keywords.explain: A set with one binary operation that is closed, associative, has an identity, and an inverse for each element.
keywords.example: (S, *) has identity 1, and 1^-1 = 1, omega^-1 = omega^2, (omega^2)^-1 = omega.

keywords.explain: Structures defined with TWO operations (+ and *); they cannot be described by a single operation alone.
keywords.example: A field like (Q, +, *) needs both addition and multiplication, unlike (S, *).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='110' y='28'>Cube roots of 1</text><circle cx='180' cy='160' r='110' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='290' cy='160' r='9' class='ok'/><text class='ink sub' x='300' y='165'>1</text><circle cx='125' cy='65' r='9' class='hi'/><text class='ink sub' x='95' y='60'>omega</text><circle cx='125' cy='255' r='9' class='hi'/><text class='ink sub' x='80' y='260'>omega^2</text><text class='ink sub' x='40' y='292'>1 * omega = omega, omega * omega^2 = 1 (closed)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The three cube roots of unity placed on the unit circle, closed under multiplication with identity 1 and mutually inverse omega and omega^2.

given.aim: Classify (S, *) where S = {1, omega, omega^2} and * is ordinary complex multiplication.

terms.meaning: The set of the three cube roots of unity.
terms.example: omega^3 = 1, so products stay inside S.
terms.connects: Closure under * is the first group axiom to check.

terms.meaning: The single binary operation, ordinary multiplication of complex numbers.
terms.connects: Only one operation is given, ruling out ring/field/domain.

terms.meaning: 1 is the multiplicative identity; every element has an inverse in S.

given.plan: Eliminate ring/field/integral domain because they need two operations, then verify the four group axioms for the single operation *.

steps.title: Rule out two-operation structures
steps.apply: Only one operation * is given. Rings, integral domains, and fields are defined with two operations (addition and multiplication), so B, C, D cannot apply.

steps.title: Verify the group axioms for (S, *)
steps.formula_raw: closure, associativity, identity e, inverse a^-1 for all a
steps.apply: Closure: products of cube roots of unity are cube roots of unity (omega*omega = omega^2, omega*omega^2 = 1). Associativity holds for complex multiplication. Identity = 1. Inverses: 1^-1=1, omega^-1=omega^2, (omega^2)^-1=omega.
steps.note: It is in fact a cyclic group of order 3 generated by omega.

solution.result: All group axioms hold under the single operation, so (S, *) is a Group; answer A.

questions.formula_note: A ring, integral domain, or field requires two operations; with a single multiplication on the cube roots of unity the structure satisfies exactly the four group axioms.

questions.question: Let G be a group with 15 elements. Let L be a subgroup of G. It is known that L != G and that the size of L is at least 4. The size of L is __________.
questions.to_find: The order of the proper subgroup L of a group of order 15, given |L| >= 4

understand.plain: By Lagrange's theorem the order of any subgroup must divide the order of the group. So |L| must be a divisor of 15. The divisors of 15 are 1, 3, 5, 15. Since L is a proper subgroup (L != G) we drop 15, and since |L| is at least 4 we drop 1 and 3. Only 5 remains.

keywords.explain: In a finite group, the order of every subgroup divides the order of the group.
keywords.example: For |G| = 15, a subgroup can only have size 1, 3, 5, or 15.

keywords.explain: A subgroup L with L != G, i.e. strictly smaller than the whole group.
keywords.example: Here L != G removes the divisor 15 from the candidates.

keywords.explain: Combine the divisibility constraint with given size bounds to pin down the order.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='14' y='28'>Divisors of |G| = 15 (possible |L|):</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='70' height='50' rx='8' class='no'/><text class='ink lbl' x='58' y='86'>1</text><rect x='130' y='55' width='70' height='50' rx='8' class='no'/><text class='ink lbl' x='158' y='86'>3</text><rect x='230' y='55' width='70' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='258' y='86'>5</text><rect x='330' y='55' width='70' height='50' rx='8' class='no'/><text class='ink lbl' x='350' y='86'>15</text><text class='mut sub' x='30' y='130'>x: 1,3 fail (need >= 4)</text><text class='mut sub' x='330' y='130'>x: 15 fails (L != G)</text><text class='ok lbl' x='225' y='150'>|L| = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The divisors of 15 are 1, 3, 5, 15; only 5 survives the constraints |L| >= 4 and L != G.

given.aim: Determine |L| given that L is a proper subgroup of a group of order 15 with at least 4 elements.

terms.meaning: The order (number of elements) of the group G.
terms.connects: |L| must be one of these divisors by Lagrange.

terms.meaning: A subgroup of G, with L != G (proper).
terms.example: L could be a subgroup of order 5.

terms.meaning: The given lower bound on the size of L.

given.plan: List the divisors of 15, then apply the proper-subgroup and size-at-least-4 conditions to find the single valid value.

steps.title: Apply Lagrange's theorem
steps.apply: Since L is a subgroup of G, |L| must divide |G| = 15. The divisors are 1, 3, 5, 15.
steps.note: These are the only candidate sizes.

steps.title: Filter by the constraints
steps.apply: L != G rules out 15. The condition |L| >= 4 rules out 1 and 3. Only 5 remains.
steps.note: Such a subgroup of order 5 indeed exists (Z_15 has one).

questions.formula_note: Lagrange's theorem forces |L| to be a divisor of 15, and the proper-subgroup plus size-at-least-4 conditions single out 5.

questions.question:
There are two elements x, y in a group (G, *) such that every element in the group can be written as a product of some number of x's and y's in some order. It is known that
  x * x = y * y = x * y * x * y = y * x * y * x = e
where e is the identity element. The maximum number of elements in such a group is _____.
questions.to_find: The maximum number of elements in the group generated by x, y under the given relations

understand.plain: The relations say x and y are each their own inverse (x*x = e, y*y = e) and that x and y commute, because (xy)(xy) = e together with x*x = y*y = e forces xy = yx. With two commuting elements of order 2, every product collapses to one of just four distinct elements: e, x, y, xy. That is the Klein four-group.

keywords.explain: An element with x*x = e, so x is its own inverse.
keywords.example: Here both x and y satisfy x^-1 = x and y^-1 = y.

keywords.explain: x*y*x*y = e with x^2 = y^2 = e gives xy = (yx)^-1 = yx, so x and y commute.
keywords.example: Multiply x*y*x*y = e on left by x and right by y: y*x = x*y.

keywords.explain: The group {e, x, y, xy} where every non-identity element has order 2; it is the largest group these relations allow.
keywords.example: x*y = y*x is the fourth element, distinct from e, x, y.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='70' y='28'>Klein four-group</text><circle cx='80' cy='90' r='30' class='ok'/><text class='ink lbl' x='72' y='96'>e</text><circle cx='240' cy='90' r='30' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='234' y='96'>x</text><circle cx='80' cy='230' r='30' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='74' y='236'>y</text><circle cx='240' cy='230' r='30' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='226' y='236'>xy</text><line x1='110' y1='90' x2='210' y2='90' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='80' y1='120' x2='80' y2='200' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='240' y1='120' x2='240' y2='200' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='110' y1='230' x2='210' y2='230' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text class='mut sub' x='40' y='285'>4 distinct elements: e, x, y, xy (each non-e has order 2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The four elements e, x, y, xy of the Klein four-group, every non-identity element being its own inverse.

given.aim: Find the largest possible order of a group generated by x, y subject to x^2 = y^2 = (xy)^2 = (yx)^2 = e.

terms.meaning: Two elements that together generate the whole group.
terms.example: Any element is a word in x and y.
terms.connects: Their relations cap how many distinct words exist.

terms.meaning: Both x and y have order 2 (are involutions).
terms.connects: Lets every word be reduced to alternating x, y.
Collapses all words to e, x, y, xy.

given.plan: Use the order-2 relations to show x and y commute, then list all reduced words, which gives exactly four distinct elements.

steps.title: x and y commute
steps.apply: From (xy)(xy) = e, xy = (xy)^-1 = y^-1 x^-1 = y*x (using x^2 = y^2 = e). So x and y commute.
steps.note: Commuting involutions generate an abelian group.

steps.title: List all distinct words
steps.formula_raw: every word reduces to one of e, x, y, xy
steps.apply: Because x^2 = y^2 = e and xy = yx, any product of x's and y's reduces to e, x, y, or xy. These are the only possible distinct elements, so at most 4 elements.

solution.result: The maximum number of elements is 4.

questions.formula_note: The relations make x and y commuting involutions, so the group collapses to the Klein four-group {e, x, y, xy} of order 4.

questions.question: Let G be a finite group on 84 elements. The size of a largest possible proper subgroup of G is _____.
questions.to_find: The largest possible order of a proper subgroup of a group of order 84

understand.plain: By Lagrange's theorem, the order of any subgroup must divide the order of the group. A proper subgroup is strictly smaller than the whole group, so we want the largest divisor of 84 that is not 84 itself. The divisors of 84 in decreasing order are 84, 42, 28, ...; the largest proper one is 42.

keywords.explain: The order of a subgroup divides the order of the finite group.
A subgroup that is not the whole group, so its order is a proper divisor of |G|.
For order n, it is n divided by the smallest prime factor of n.
keywords.example: 84 = 2 x 42, and 2 is the smallest prime factor, so 42 is the largest proper divisor.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='30' y='30'>G : |G| = 84</text><circle cx='180' cy='110' r='80' class='bg'/><text class='ink lbl' x='150' y='55'>|G|=84</text><circle cx='180' cy='130' r='52' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='150' y='136'>|H|=42</text><text class='mut sub' x='40' y='190'>largest proper subgroup = 84 / (smallest prime) = 84/2 = 42</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A group of order 84 containing a proper subgroup of the largest possible order 42, which is 84 divided by its smallest prime factor 2.

given.aim: Find the maximum order of a proper subgroup H of a group G with |G| = 84.

terms.meaning: Number of elements of the group.

terms.example: |H| is a divisor of 84 strictly less than 84.
terms.connects: Maximizing |H| means taking the largest proper divisor.
Gives the maximal proper subgroup size.

given.plan: Apply Lagrange's theorem and take the largest proper divisor of 84, which is 84 divided by its smallest prime factor.

steps.title: Subgroup order divides 84
steps.apply: By Lagrange, |H| must be a divisor of 84. The divisors are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 12, 14, 21, 28, 42, 84.

steps.title: Take the largest proper divisor
steps.apply: The smallest prime factor of 84 is 2, so the largest proper divisor is 84 / 2 = 42. Such a subgroup of index 2 can exist (e.g. in Z_84).
steps.note: 42 is the maximum proper subgroup size.

solution.result: The largest possible proper subgroup has 42 elements.

questions.formula_note: Lagrange's theorem restricts subgroup orders to divisors of 84, and the largest proper divisor is 84 divided by its smallest prime factor 2, giving 42.

questions.question:
Let G be an arbitrary group. Consider the following relations on G:
  R1: For all a, b in G, a R1 b if and only if there exists g in G such that a = g^(-1) * b * g
  R2: For all a, b in G, a R2 b if and only if a = b^(-1)
Which of the above is/are equivalence relation/relations?
A. R1 and R2
B. R1 only
C. R2 only
D. Neither R1 nor R2
questions.to_find: Which of R1 (conjugacy) and R2 (a = b inverse) are equivalence relations

understand.plain: An equivalence relation must be reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. R1 is the conjugacy relation a = g^-1 b g; conjugacy is a classic equivalence relation. R2 says a = b^-1; this fails reflexivity in general because a = a^-1 only when a has order 1 or 2, so most elements are not related to themselves. Hence only R1 is an equivalence relation.

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive (a R a), symmetric (a R b => b R a), and transitive (a R b, b R c => a R c).
keywords.example: Conjugacy R1 satisfies all three on any group.

keywords.explain: a is conjugate to b if a = g^-1 b g for some g; it partitions G into conjugacy classes.
keywords.example: Reflexive with g = e: a = e^-1 a e = a.

keywords.explain: a R2 a means a = a^-1, i.e. a^2 = e, which is false for elements of order > 2.
keywords.example: In Z_4, element 1 has 1^-1 = 3 != 1, so 1 is not R2-related to itself.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text class='ink lbl' x='40' y='28'>R1: a = g^(-1) b g</text><text class='ink lbl' x='250' y='28'>R2: a = b^(-1)</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='180' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='240' y='50' width='160' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><text class='ok sub' x='40' y='80'>Reflexive (g=e)</text><text class='ok sub' x='40' y='105'>Symmetric</text><text class='ok sub' x='40' y='130'>Transitive</text><text class='ok lbl' x='40' y='158'>=> equivalence</text><text class='no sub' x='258' y='80'>Reflexive? a=a^(-1)?</text><text class='no sub' x='258' y='105'>fails if order > 2</text><text class='no lbl' x='258' y='140'>=> not equivalence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: R1 (conjugacy) is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive so it is an equivalence relation; R2 (a = b inverse) fails reflexivity, so only R1 qualifies.

given.aim: Decide which of R1 (conjugacy) and R2 (a equals b inverse) is an equivalence relation on an arbitrary group G.

terms.meaning: The conjugacy relation; a and b are related if conjugate by some g.
terms.example: Taking g = e gives a = a, so reflexive.
terms.connects: Conjugacy is the standard equivalence relation partitioning G.

terms.meaning: a is related to b exactly when a is the inverse of b.
terms.example: a R2 a needs a = a^-1, i.e. a^2 = e.
terms.connects: Reflexivity fails whenever some element has order > 2.

terms.meaning: The three properties required for an equivalence relation.
terms.example: R1 has all three; R2 lacks reflexivity (and transitivity).
terms.connects: Only relations passing all three count.

given.plan: Test reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity for each relation; keep those satisfying all three.

steps.title: Check R1 (conjugacy)
steps.apply: Reflexive: a = e^-1 a e. Symmetric: a = g^-1 b g => b = (g^-1)^-1 a (g^-1). Transitive: a = g^-1 b g, b = h^-1 c h => a = (hg)^-1 c (hg). All three hold.

steps.title: Check R2 (a = b^-1)
steps.formula_raw: reflexive: a R a requires a = a^-1
steps.apply: Reflexivity needs a = a^-1, i.e. a^2 = e, which fails for any element of order > 2 (e.g. in Z_4). So R2 is not reflexive and hence not an equivalence relation.
steps.note: R2 also fails transitivity in general.

solution.result: Only R1 is an equivalence relation, so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Conjugacy R1 satisfies reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity, while R2 (a = b^-1) fails reflexivity because a = a^-1 holds only for elements of order at most 2.

formulas.name: Order of an Element
formulas.plain: order(x) = smallest positive integer k with x^k = e
formulas.whenToUse: Use to measure how many times an element must be multiplied by itself to return to the identity; central to deciding if an element generates a cyclic group.

terms.means: the identity element of the group
the least positive power giving the identity

formulas.trap: The order of an element divides the order of the group; an element of order 1 is just the identity, never a generator of a larger group.

formulas.name: Generators of a Cyclic Group
formulas.plain: an element generates a finite cyclic group exactly when its order equals the group's order
formulas.whenToUse: Use to identify which elements of a finite cyclic group are generators; equivalently, in Z_n the generators are the integers coprime to n.
formulas.trap: A cyclic group of order n has exactly phi(n) generators, not just one; for n = 4 there are phi(4) = 2 generators.

formulas.name: Group Axioms
formulas.plain: a set with one operation is a group if it is closed, associative, has an identity e, and every element has an inverse
formulas.whenToUse: Use to verify whether a set with a single binary operation forms a group, and to distinguish a group from two-operation structures like rings and fields.

terms.means: identity element with e*a = a*e = a
inverse of a, with a*a^{-1} = e

formulas.trap: Rings, integral domains, and fields require two operations; a structure with only one operation can be a group but never a ring/field.

formulas.name: Lagrange's Theorem
formulas.plain: the order of any subgroup divides the order of the finite group
formulas.whenToUse: Use to restrict possible subgroup sizes and to compute indices; the order of every element (which equals the order of the cyclic subgroup it generates) also divides |G|.

terms.means: index of H in G, equal to |G|/|H|

formulas.trap: The converse is false: a divisor d of |G| need not give a subgroup of order d (Lagrange guarantees only necessity, not existence).

formulas.name: Largest Proper Subgroup Size
formulas.latex: \max\{|H| : H < G\} = \dfrac{|G|}{p},\ p = \text{smallest prime factor of } |G|
formulas.plain: the largest possible proper subgroup order is |G| divided by the smallest prime factor of |G|
formulas.whenToUse: Use to find the maximum size of a proper subgroup: it is the largest proper divisor of |G|, namely |G| divided by its least prime factor.
formulas.trap: This gives the largest divisor that could be a subgroup order; existence of a subgroup of that exact order is guaranteed for abelian/cyclic groups but is the maximal achievable bound by Lagrange in general.

formulas.name: Commuting Involutions Generate the Klein Four-Group
formulas.latex: x^2 = y^2 = (xy)^2 = e \Rightarrow xy = yx,\ \langle x,y\rangle = \{e,x,y,xy\}
formulas.plain: two order-2 elements whose product also has order 2 commute, generating a group of at most 4 elements
formulas.whenToUse: Use when generators each square to the identity and their product squares to the identity; the relations force commutativity and cap the group at the Klein four-group.

terms.means: the two generators, each of order 2
the product, the fourth distinct element

formulas.trap: Without the relation (xy)^2 = e the two involutions need not commute and can generate a dihedral group with many more elements.

formulas.name: Equivalence Relation
formulas.latex: \text{reflexive: } aRa;\ \text{symmetric: } aRb\Rightarrow bRa;\ \text{transitive: } aRb \wedge bRc \Rightarrow aRc
formulas.plain: a relation is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive
formulas.whenToUse: Use to test whether a relation partitions a set into equivalence classes; all three properties must hold.

terms.means: arbitrary elements of the underlying set
reflexivity: every element relates to itself

formulas.trap: Failing even one property disqualifies the relation; conjugacy is an equivalence relation, but a = b^-1 fails reflexivity unless every element has order at most 2.
